efficacyPairs.questionText: What comes next in this letter series? A, C, E, G, ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Let's split **AC, CE, EG, GI**. When letters skip one, each row jumps by **2 positions** instead of 1:
lessonLearningGoal: Spot when letters skip one position — jumping by 2 instead of 1
Use Split and Track with +2 or -2 step patterns confidently

efficacyPairs.questionText: What comes next in this series? AZ, BY, CX, DW, ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Let's split **AZ, BY, CX, DW**. The two letters are heading in **opposite** directions — this is the most common pattern in letter pair series (a list of letter pairs that follow a rule)!
lessonLearningGoal: Spot the twist: one letter goes forward while the other goes backward!
See why Split and Track is essential when letters head in opposite directions

efficacyPairs.questionText: What comes next in this letter series? B, D, F, H, ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Let's split **AC, CE, EG, GI**. When letters skip one, each row jumps by **2 positions** instead of 1:

Use Split and Track with +2 or -2 step patterns confidently

efficacyPairs.questionText: What comes next? A, B, D, G, ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Let's split **AF, BH, CJ, DL**. The key: each row can have its **own step size**. Don't assume they're the same!
lessonLearningGoal: Handle the trickiest type: when each row uses a different step size
See why Split and Track always works, even when the steps don't match

efficacyPairs.questionText: What comes next in this series? AC, BD, CE, DF, ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Look at **AD, BE, CF, DG** — every pair has a gap of **3** between its letters. You can use this to double-check your answer:
lessonLearningGoal: Discover the constant gap — a brilliant shortcut hiding inside many series
Use the gap as a quick double-check to make sure your answer is right
